title: Diplacus jepsonii
taxonomy: Diplacus jepsonii, also known as Jepson's monkey flower, is a species of flowering plant in the family Scrophulariaceae. It belongs to the genus Diplacus, which is a group of flowering plants native to the western coast of North America. The plant is further classified as a member of the order Lamiales, a large and diverse group of flowering plants that include mint, lavender, and snapdragon. The species is also a part of the family Scrophulariaceae, which includes plants such as foxglove and toadflax. At the kingdom level, Diplacus jepsonii is classified as a member of the kingdom Plantae.

Its roots and leaves have been used to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions. intro: Diplacus jepsonii, also known as Jepson's monkey flower, is a perennial plant species belonging to the family Lamiaceae, characterized by its bright orange or yellow flowers with distinctive monkey-like markings. The plant is valued for its potential health benefits, including its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which may aid in reducing pain and inflammation, as well as supporting immune function. Bioactive constituents of Diplacus jepsonii include phenolic compounds, flavonoids, and terpenoids, such as jepsonone and isojepsonone, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The plant is commonly used in herbal preparations, including teas, tinctures, and capsules, often in combination with other herbs to enhance its therapeutic effects and support overall well-being.
